questions.question: Which Spring scope corresponds to ASP.NET Core's AddTransient - a new instance every time the bean is injected?
questions.explanation: Prototype creates a new instance for every injection or getBean call, matching AddTransient. Note that prototype-scoped beans don't get @PreDestroy callbacks - Spring doesn't track them after construction.

questions.question: You inject a request-scoped bean into a singleton service via a normal constructor. What goes wrong?
choices: Nothing - Spring handles cross-scope injection automatically.
Either the application fails to start (no active request at construction time) or the singleton holds the first request's instance for the rest of the app's lifetime - a subtle but serious bug.
The request-scoped bean is duplicated per-thread.
Spring serialises requests through the singleton to keep the bean valid.
questions.explanation: Singletons hold their dependencies for the app's lifetime. Without a scoped proxy, the request-scoped bean either can't be resolved at startup or freezes to the first request's instance. The fix is proxyMode = TARGET_CLASS (or INTERFACES) so Spring injects a proxy that resolves the current request's instance per call.

questions.question: When does @PostConstruct fire for a singleton bean?
choices: Just before the bean is destroyed.
After the bean is constructed and its dependencies have been injected, before the application context is considered fully started.
Only when called explicitly via reflection.
questions.explanation: @PostConstruct runs once, after construction and dependency injection complete, as part of the bean's initialization phase. It's the standard hook for one-time setup like cache warming or registration. For startup work that depends on the full context being ready, prefer ApplicationRunner.
